static_analysis.description:
Reentrancy in Reentrancy.withdraw() (contracts/eth/re.sol#17-27):
	External calls:
	- (sent) = msg.sender.call{value: bal}() (contracts/eth/re.sol#21)
	State variables written after the call(s):
	- balances[msg.sender] = 0 (contracts/eth/re.sol#24)
	Reentrancy.balances (contracts/eth/re.sol#11) can be used in cross function reentrancies:
	- Reentrancy.balances (contracts/eth/re.sol#11)
	- Reentrancy.deposit() (contracts/eth/re.sol#13-15)
	- Reentrancy.withdraw() (contracts/eth/re.sol#17-27)

EchidnaTest.isAttacking (contracts/eth/re.sol#39) is written in both
	isAttacking = true (contracts/eth/re.sol#55)
	isAttacking = false (contracts/eth/re.sol#57)

Reentrancy in EchidnaTest.test_withdraw() (contracts/eth/re.sol#53-59):
	External calls:
	- withdraw() (contracts/eth/re.sol#56)
		- (sent) = msg.sender.call{value: bal}() (contracts/eth/re.sol#21)
	State variables written after the call(s):
	- isAttacking = false (contracts/eth/re.sol#57)
Pragma version^0.8.28 (contracts/eth/re.sol#2) necessitates a version too recent to be trusted. Consider deploying with 0.6.12/0.7.6/0.8.16
solc-0.8.28 is not recommended for deployment

Low level call in Reentrancy.withdraw() (contracts/eth/re.sol#17-27):
	- (sent) = msg.sender.call{value: bal}() (contracts/eth/re.sol#21)
Function EchidnaTest.test_withdraw() (contracts/eth/re.sol#53-59) is not in mixedCase
Function EchidnaTest.test_deposit() (contracts/eth/re.sol#45-50) is not in mixedCase

A call to a user-supplied address is executed.
An external message call to an address specified by the caller is executed. Note that the callee account might contain arbitrary code and could re-enter any function within this contract. Reentering the contract in an intermediate state may lead to unexpected behaviour. Make sure that no state modifications are executed after this call and/or reentrancy guards are in place.

The value of the call is dependent on balance or storage write
This can lead to race conditions. An attacker may be able to run a transaction after our transaction which can change the value of the call

Write to persistent state following external call
The contract account state is accessed after an external call to a user defined address. To prevent reentrancy issues, consider accessing the state only before the call, especially if the callee is untrusted. Alternatively, a reentrancy lock can be used to prevent untrusted callees from re-entering the contract in an intermediate state.
